item.title: Re: Image source
item.content:encoded: Because of this, I've removed the image for being non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: Image source
item.content:encoded:
the image is an artwork by [Ember / MC] Barrett.

http://projectwaldo.blogspot.com/2011/06/chasing-windmills.html
https://dopemagazine.com/ember-barrett-art/

While rummaging through the remains of Ms Barrett's site on archive.org, I saw no indication that she had made any of her work available under Creative Commons or another suitable license.
Another great milestone for another great article.

item.title: Some nice fridge horror for y'all:
item.content:encoded:
SCP-407 also involves a whole ecosystem evolving from a deceased D-class's gut flora. In that case, it's because of a song that induces rapid cell growth. Testing of 407 was eventually limited to just the first 20 minutes of the song—past that point, a Cordyceps-looking fungus infested and killed everything in the ecosystem. And SCP-507 showed that this fungus, or one very similar, destroyed the Foundation in an alternate universe.

So, keep up the good work containing SCP-1859, everyone. No pressure.

item.title: Physics consistency nit-pick
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the most fascinating skips purely from a life-sciences perspective. Really well thought out, and I appreciate the final note as one of those "inspired scientist" moments. I doubt the Foundation cares, but if Dr. P████████ is feeling it and has the clearance, I totally get why they let him spout off like that.

One problem though
Why do the plants wilt when the light is shown inward?

I thought it was a little off on my first reading, and the more I thought about it the less sense it made. As the visible light shines in and time gets sped up, the light would get red-shifted and its irradiance would be reduced (fewer photons per unit of subjective time). All that would do is provide a dull heat early on, and a very weak radio-wave beyond that. You'd probably see some accelerated ecological succession as the plants adapt to a (very slightly) warmer environment.

Even it it happens to absorb 100% of the radiant energy as heat (given some black-body resonance thing I might be making up) it would still not be enough. The batteries of the flashlight provides even less wattage to our heat-ray since the energy input is being spread over a very long period of time. If anything were to whither the plants I think it would be when the flashlight it turned outward, since the light hits the plants at both increased frequency and increased irradiance. I think, though, that the short duration of a sped-up glance would make it pretty benign.

TLDR love the skip, and will forgive the error for the opportunity to watch a fantastical ecological catastrophe. This well-aged skip has my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you ever read The Sick Land? This reminds me a bit of that.

item.title: Show Flah's masterpiece some love!!
item.content:encoded: Coming back here to say his needs more attention. Last comment was in 2015? Come ooooon peoples, this is probably one of the coolest and creepily plausible sciencey Skips we have here!

item.title: Exterminated?
item.content:encoded: The picture says the creatures were exterminated, but I didn't find any mention of that in the article itself. Did they just die of radiation poisoning? Is that what you meant by "exterminated"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read this one a while back, but wanted to say this is definitely one of my favorite Skips around, if not my most favorite. This story is incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another skip introduced into my personal Hall of Favorites. Spectacular job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, two upvotes in a moment for an article we like? No thanking needed. : D

Edit: Besides, that I read this one is technically your fault. I am reading The Deep End. : P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you Dr Reach for bringing this one back to my attention so I could upvote it. This was one of the ones I read before I joined. It helped me fall in love with this site.

+1, well deserved. Awesome temporospatial/biological anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… this is my new favorite SCP article.

Unexpected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
COOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOLLLLLLLLLLLLLLL!
Seriously, this is fantastic. You gave me a science boner. I'm beyond words with how good and cool this is. I want this to be a real place.

SCIENCE
SCIENCE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, the third note is really out of place and out of tone for a SCP entry, but I don't actually have a problem with it. It does help giving this a nice mood.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went from "oh shit" to heartstring-tugged. The last note is a bit too sentimental for this site, and reads more like a middle school textbook or travel guide or diary than a scientific report, but I really like it nonetheless. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing is amazing. You very clearly researched the science behind this, and for me, the final note doesn't ruin it, but puts into words those things that maybe we're all thinking about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the most awesome articles on the website.

Great detail and good scientific knowledge. This is the Foundation at it's finest!

Totally upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What everybody else said, but like, wittier and more insightful and stuff.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is fucking outstanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this except for the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would too.

One of my favorite SCP's on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing. I would read a book-length version of this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, shit. This puts my blueshift anomaly to shame, but is the best piece of (essentially) batshit crazy science I've ever seen. It takes an absolutely berzerk-insane idea, and rigorously plays out the consequences. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That third note was the only thing I disliked about this. I'd advise just cutting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Argh! I had a half-written temporal anomaly SCP involving redshifting. Now it'll just look derivative :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. I don't know how else to put it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See this? You see this Hollywood? This is what happens when someone's writing the script to Evolution, and actually pays attention to the science going on!

(This is a good thing. +1 all around good sir)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This worked out well. I still don't like that bloody picture, but then I just complain alot. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The time shift goes the other way. Time goes quicker, not slower. If time went slower, we'd be dealing with a Black Hole-like effect with the radiation red-shifting as it goes outwards. This acts more like a White Hole. Unfortunately, the English language still isn't equipped to handle different rates of time flow, so confusion can still happen.

As for the resonance frequency, physics is already a little bit screwed up inside there. I think I'll tweak that sentence a bit, though. Change it so that it's consistent with the resonance frequency, rather than being created from it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This deserves every upvote it gets. You worked very hard on this and it shows. Keep it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man I remember seeing an early draft of this on the forums. You've only improved it. I have a feeling the final note is going to be a sticking point with a lot of people (it does feel overly dramatic), but you've got an upvote from me. I'm very glad to see this on the site proper now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see what causes the high rate of evolution for things in the Green zone. Not only does time move slower at the center of the cavern, but external sources of ionizing radiation are red-shifted into non-ionizing radiation, further reducing the rate of mutation.

In the case of SCP-1859, calculations show that the cause of the high energy radiation is the primary resonance frequency of electromagnetic radition in the chamber itself (approximately ███ Hz).

Resonance doesn't actually produce energy. If there was a directional asymmetry to the wavelength shifting, such that light was red-shifted to a lesser magnitude going out that it's blue-shifted going in, that would create an endless source of energy as photons were bounced back and forth from the center to edge and back.

Though the rate of photon production in this manner is very low, the compression of time ultimately results in lethal doses of ionizing radiation at certain distances.

Since time is slowed in the center of the cavern compared to the edges, some on the edge would perceive the center to be producing photons at a slower rate. Time compression would only be observed by someone in the center looking out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hoo boy… You have no idea how many revisions I went through before I even found a format that worked. I'm glad that the effort is appreciated.

Now to make sure it doesn't all get to my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. There are a couple of places where the use of "incredible" or "incredibly" seems to break the objective tone you've maintained throughout, but this is a minor nitpick.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP has a lot of great stuff. Honestly the what would really help, though, would be a diagram of the layout of the SCP if you could find or make one—the containment procedures are good but it's hard to visualize the zones and access points without having read the description. Upvoted, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.
Honestly, what sealed this for me was not the core concept, but the details. It looks like you put
lots of time and effort into writing this, and the tone of writing is excellent. Complex, detailed SCPs like this are fun to read, and you pulled it off quite well.

Lines I especially like:

five hours (subjective time)

This tips you off to the temporal part of the SCP in a way that's rather subtle and made me go: "Wait, what? Oh,
where exactly is this going?" This is always good in any area of writing. I wanted to keep reading it.

D-Class personnel assigned to an expedition must first be screened for any background suggesting that they are aware of the symptoms of radiation poisoning.

I always like it when the moral ambiguity of the Foundation is shown. Yes, they would check that the D-class were
unfamiliar with the symptoms of radiation poisoning for the sole purpose of sending them in to explore an area that
would irradiate them. They're expendable.

I enjoyed how you showed the progression of the exploration over time (they didn't know there was life there at first), and the notes at the bottom add "flavor".

The use of more clinical-sounding terms (like "monitoring station" instead of, say, "camera") and the way you
described radiation made it sound like an actual scientific report. I don't know how to really say this other than "THAT SOUNDS SCIENCY, OOH".

All in all, good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. The science is impressive. You put a lot of work into this, and it shows. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I have read so far is very impressive but I need to rest up and give this thing a good going over to really appreciate it. That's not a complaint, I'm just too fresh out of bed on too few hours of sleep to give this the attention I want to right now. X_X

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please read the Attribute Tag Guide for information on what are now "acceptable" tags. :) Your help in this project is appreciated!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shitton of things happening, and somehow it isn't bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is spectacularly detailed. That final note reads more like an over-dramatic documentary narration than creepypasta, but it's not entirely out of place.

There's a ridiculous number of things going on in this SCP, but you manage to tie them together and make it legitimately academically interesting. I've seen SCPs that win by creep, by horror, by humor, and by oddity, but this may be the only thing I've seen that wins by science alone. Impressive work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this a lot. Upvoted hard.

EDIT: I derped. Hard.

I would suggest simply calling the Black/Green Zone the Green Zone for simplicity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have been told that the final note by Dr P reads a bit too much like bad Creepypasta. I would like other opinions and suggestions on how this can be improved. If you have other objections to anything in the entry, please feel free to rip it a new one so that I can correct it.
